statement.markdown: Given an undirected graph, return true if and only if it is bipartite. A graph is bipartite if and only if it is 2-colorable. A graph is 2-colorable if and only if it does not contain any odd-length cycle. A bipartite graph is a graph whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets U and V such that every edge connects a vertex in U to one in V. In other words, the graph is 2-colorable, i.e., its chromatic number is 2.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer representing the number of nodes (n) in the graph.
The second line contains a single integer representing the number of edges (m) in the graph.
For each edge, there are two lines: one containing the source node (u) and one containing the destination node (v).
statement.outputFormat: A single line containing 'true' if the graph is bipartite, and 'false' otherwise.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the graph.
The number of edges in the graph.

examples.explanation: The graph can be divided into two disjoint sets U = {1, 3} and V = {2, 4} such that every edge connects a vertex in U to one in V.
The graph contains an odd-length cycle (1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 1), so it is not bipartite.

hints.text: To determine if a graph is bipartite, consider using a graph traversal algorithm to assign colors to each node.
The graph traversal algorithm can be used to try to color the graph with two colors such that no two adjacent nodes have the same color.
A depth-first search (DFS) approach can be used to recursively assign colors to each node and check for any conflicts.

editorial.title: Bipartite Graph Verification via DFS
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by verifying if a given graph is bipartite, i.e., its vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that every edge connects two vertices from different sets. A graph is bipartite if and only if it does not contain a cycle of odd length.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking all possible color assignments for the nodes. However, this approach is impractical due to its exponential time complexity. The provided DFS solution is an optimized approach that leverages the properties of bipartite graphs.
editorial.betterSolution: The DFS solution works by assigning colors to nodes as it traverses the graph. If a node has not been colored yet, it assigns a color to the node and recursively checks its neighbors. If a neighbor has the same color as the current node, or if a conflict is found during the recursion, the graph is not bipartite.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution utilizes a DFS traversal with a time complexity of O(N + M) and a space complexity of O(N + M), where N is the number of nodes and M is the number of edges. This approach is efficient because it only visits each node and edge once.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the DFS solution can be proven by observing that a graph is bipartite if and only if it is 2-colorable. The DFS traversal ensures that if a node can be colored with one color, all its neighbors will be colored with the other color. If at any point a node is found to have the same color as one of its neighbors, the graph is not bipartite.
editorial.dryRun: To verify the solution, one can perform a dry run with test cases, including graphs known to be bipartite and non-bipartite. For instance, a graph with nodes {1, 2, 3, 4} and edges {(1, 2), (1, 3), (2, 4), (3, 4)} is bipartite.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to update the color of a node during DFS
Incorrectly assuming a graph is bipartite without thorough verification
Not considering the case where a node has not been colored yet
Not properly handling recursion limits for large graphs
